one_line_takeaway: A training-free, black-box multi-turn jailbreak pipeline that pairs a self-improving pattern-memory with adaptive temperature control to reach up to 95% ASR on Llama-3.1-8B (99% on Qwen3-235B), empirically showing single-turn alignment fails to hold across extended conversations.
key_finding: AutoAdv achieves up to 95% ASR on Llama-3.1-8B within six turns, a 24% absolute improvement over single-turn baselines. Multi-turn consistently beats single-turn across all four targets, with gains concentrated in early turns and diminishing marginal benefit later. Vulnerability varies widely by model: Qwen3-235B was most susceptible (99% ASR over six turns) while GPT-4o-mini was most resilient (86% ASR over six turns). Ablations on Llama-3.1-8B (6 turns) show every component contributes, with few-shot exemplars being the single most important: removing them drops ASR from 95% to 78%; removing seed strategies -> 86%; disabling the pattern manager -> 89%; disabling the temperature manager -> 88%. The overall conclusion is that single-turn safety evaluations substantially underestimate real vulnerability, and alignment optimized for single-turn robustness degrades over extended conversations.
core_contribution: AutoAdv is a training-free, black-box framework for automated MULTI-TURN jailbreaking of aligned LLMs. Its own novelty is the combination of three adaptive mechanisms driving an iterative, feedback-driven prompt-rewriting loop: (1) a Pattern Manager that records which rewriting techniques produced successful jailbreaks (against a taxonomy of 28 predefined categories detected via keyword matching), ranks them with a weighted multi-factor scoring function, and re-injects the top-5 techniques plus up to 3 concrete example prompts into the attacker LLM's system prompt, persisting this learned knowledge across sessions via JSON storage; (2) a Temperature Manager that dynamically tunes the attacker's sampling temperature within [0.1, 1.5] (T0=0.7) using four priority-ordered strategies (Reset, Oscillating, Progressive, Adaptive) selected based on recent failure scores; and (3) a two-phase rewriting strategy where an initial rewrite disguises the harmful request and adaptive follow-up rewrites diagnose refusal cues and iteratively refine until success or N turns. Paper's OWN Table 1 positions AutoAdv as the only listed framework combining all of: multi-turn, pattern manager, temperature manager, training-free, and adaptive prompting (Crescendo, Tempest, GOAT, AutoDAN-Turbo, PAIR, AdvPrompter, Rainbow Teaming are CITED comparison work, not this paper's contribution).
threat_model: Black-box adversary with only API-level query access to a target LLM (no weights, no gradients). The attacker automates the role of a human red-teamer who adaptively rephrases a harmful seed request across a multi-turn conversation until safety alignment is bypassed. An attacker LLM (Grok 3 Mini, chosen for high task compliance and comparatively weak safety alignment) generates the adversarial rewrites; a separate evaluator LLM (GPT-4o-mini) scores whether the target complied. Goal is to elicit harmful/restricted content (violence, weapons, hacking, fraud, drug synthesis, misinformation, harassment) while maintaining a benign surface. The paper's framing is offensive/red-teaming for constructive security research, motivated by the gap that alignment tuned for single-turn interactions fails to hold across extended multi-turn dialogues.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section restricts all three roles (attacker, target, evaluator) to API-accessible LLMs, and states the rewriting strategies do not cover the full attack space (no multimodal or cross-lingual jailbreaks). Reviewer-observed additional caveats: jailbreak 'success' is defined entirely by an LLM-judge (GPT-4o-mini) StrongREJECT-style score > 0.5, so results depend on that judge's calibration and no human validation of ASR is reported; pattern detection is keyword-based against a fixed 28-category taxonomy, which can mislabel techniques; evaluation samples only 100 prompts per batch (50+50) from a 700-prompt pool and the number of batches/seeds and variance/confidence intervals are not reported; the attacker deliberately relies on a weakly aligned model (Grok 3 Mini), so transferability of the automated pipeline to other attacker models is untested; and comparisons to prior frameworks are qualitative (a capability checklist in Table 1) rather than head-to-head ASR on a shared benchmark.

datasets_used: AdvBench (500 prompts, ~71% of pool; violence, fraud, hacking categories)
HarmBench (200 prompts, ~29% of pool; harassment, misinformation, privacy categories)
Combined 700-prompt pool; each test batch = 100 prompts randomly sampled (50 AdvBench + 50 HarmBench)
quantitative_results: Up to 95% ASR on Llama-3.1-8B within 6 turns
24% improvement of multi-turn over single-turn baselines
Qwen3-235B: 99% ASR over 6 turns (most vulnerable target)
GPT-4o-mini: 86% ASR over 6 turns (most resilient target)
Ablation on Llama-3.1-8B (6 turns): full learning 95%; no few-shot examples 78%; no seed strategies 86%; no pattern manager 89%; no temperature manager 88%
Success threshold tau = 0.5 on unified score Sscore = (C + L + 5*(1-R))/15
Temperature range [0.1, 1.5], initial T0 = 0.7, per-step change capped at 30% of range
Pattern taxonomy of 28 categories (18 core + 10 extended); top-5 techniques + up to 3 examples injected per run
Sample dialogue: success achieved at turn 3 with evaluation score 0.800
baselines_compared: Single-turn attack baseline (AutoAdv's own single-turn variant) - primary quantitative comparison (multi-turn +24%)
Ablated AutoAdv variants (no pattern manager / no temperature manager / no seed strategies / no few-shot / baseline) - internal ablation
Qualitative capability comparison in Table 1 vs cited frameworks Crescendo, Tempest, GOAT, AutoDAN-Turbo, PAIR, AdvPrompter, Rainbow Teaming (no shared-benchmark ASR head-to-head reported)
